quiz.name: Session 09 Final Quiz: SOC Workflow and Automation
quiz.intro: 9 multiple-choice questions assessing session learning outcomes. Select the single best answer for each question.

questiontext.text: Which of the following alert closure classifications should trigger a rule tuning review?

answer.text: True Positive — Escalated
False Positive — rule incorrectly fired

feedback.text: Correct. When a rule fires incorrectly (false positive), the rule needs to be reviewed and refined to reduce noise.

answer.text: Benign True Positive — authorized activity
True Positive — Contained

questiontext.text: Your SOAR playbook automatically isolates any host where the AV detects malware. A production database server triggers the detection (a false positive from a new AV signature). The server is automatically isolated and 500 users lose database access for 2 hours. What design flaw caused this?

answer.text: The SOAR platform was incorrectly configured
The AV signature database was out of date
No whitelist of critical servers was configured for automatic isolation actions

feedback.text: Correct. Destructive automation actions must have a whitelist of critical assets that are excluded from automatic isolation to prevent business impact from false positives.

answer.text: The isolation action should have used a different API endpoint

questiontext.text: What is the difference between MTTD and MTTR?

answer.text: MTTD measures false positive rate; MTTR measures true positive rate
MTTD measures network detection speed; MTTR measures endpoint recovery time
MTTD measures the time from attack start to detection; MTTR measures the time from detection to resolution

feedback.text: Correct. Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) and Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) are two separate metrics measuring different phases of incident response.

answer.text: They measure the same thing but for different severity levels

questiontext.text: In a SOAR playbook, which type of action should ALWAYS require human approval before execution?

answer.text: Querying VirusTotal for an IP address
Creating a ticket in the case management system
Sending a notification email to the SOC team
Blocking an IP address at the perimeter firewall

feedback.text: Correct. Firewall blocking is a destructive action that can cause business disruption (e.g., blocking a CDN IP). It must always require human approval.

questiontext.text: A SOC team's P2 SLA is 4 hours MTTR. In the past month, 12 out of 60 P2 cases breached the SLA. What is the SLA compliance rate?
A security case has been in PENDING status for 6 hours waiting for a system owner to confirm a remote login. No response has been received. Which action is most appropriate?

answer.text: Close the case as a false positive
Continue waiting in PENDING status indefinitely
Escalate to Tier 3 for advanced analysis
Attempt direct contact through an alternative channel (phone/Teams); escalate to Tier 2 if still no response

feedback.text: Correct. Passive waiting is not acceptable when an SLA may be at risk. Analysts must escalate through alternative channels and eventually to a higher tier.

questiontext.text: Which of the following is the best description of a SOAR playbook?

answer.text: A database of known attack signatures used for detection
A formal contract between the SOC and the business defining response times
A log analysis query used in SIEM to correlate events
A documented, automated workflow that responds to a specific type of security alert

feedback.text: Correct. A playbook is a repeatable, automated workflow triggered by a specific alert type that executes enrichment, decision logic, and response actions.

questiontext.text: A playbook automatically blocks a source IP at the firewall if a brute force alert fires and the VirusTotal score is above 3. A legitimate authorized penetration test from an external firm is automatically blocked, disrupting a scheduled engagement. What automation pitfall does this illustrate?

answer.text: Incorrect API integration between SOAR and the firewall
Insufficient threat intelligence data from VirusTotal
Missing an exclusion list for authorized/known sources before executing destructive actions

feedback.text: Correct. All destructive automation actions must check an exclusion list (authorized pen-test IPs, maintenance windows, known partner ranges) before executing, to prevent false positive blocking of legitimate activity.

answer.text: The playbook trigger threshold was set too low

questiontext.text: A playbook is triggered by a phishing email detection. It extracts URLs, checks them against a threat feed, and creates a TheHive case for analyst review. No automated blocking or containment occurs. Which playbook type is this?

answer.text: Preventive
Detective

feedback.text: Correct. A detective playbook gathers intelligence and surfaces findings to an analyst without taking containment or blocking action. Preventive playbooks block before impact; responsive playbooks contain and remediate after detection.

answer.text: Responsive
Hybrid preventive-responsive
